Q1: I tried to keep a somewhat regular study routine, usually blocking out time in the afternoons and evenings when I could focus. With nine online courses, I had to be strategic about it, using visual schedules and color-coded calendars to keep track of everything. Some weeks were more chaotic than others, especially when my anxiety spiked and I'd lose a day just trying to calm down. I aimed for about two hours a day across different subjects, but honestly, it didn't always work out that neatly.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and how I was feeling mentally. I'd try to set regular times, but when stress hit hard, everything would shift. Closer to due dates, I'd find myself cramming more hours in, which wasn't ideal but it's how I survived. The anxiety made it hard to stick to any strict schedule because some days I just couldn't focus no matter what I tried.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new assignments or announcements that got me to log in. I'd also go in to watch lecture videos since I'm a visual learner and those helped me understand concepts better than just reading. Sometimes I logged in just to make sure I hadn't missed anything important because the fear of falling behind was always there. My peer support group would also remind me to check certain things, which helped.
Q4: It took a few weeks, but eventually logging in became part of my morning routine along with coffee. The peer support intervention actually helped with this because we'd check in with each other about what we'd seen on the LMS. Having that accountability made it feel less like a chore and more like something I just did. Though I'll admit, during high-stress periods, even that routine would fall apart temporarily.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best visually. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing and rewinding to really absorb the material. The discussion forums were also surprisingly helpful because seeing how other students approached problems gave me new perspectives. I also found the peer interaction features useful, especially after joining the support group where we'd share resources and explain things to each other.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary reading materials because they felt overwhelming when I was already juggling so much. Some of the optional quizzes I'd delay until the last minute or not do at all because they weren't graded. I know I should have engaged more with those, but when you're stressed and anxious, you prioritize what's absolutely necessary. The lengthy PDF documents especially got ignored because staring at walls of text just doesn't work for my brain.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. Visual content like diagrams, videos, and interactive elements always felt more valuable to me. If something was clearly going to be on an exam or was required for an assignment, I'd prioritize it. The peer discussions also felt worthwhile because they reduced my anxiety about being the only one confused.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. With required work, there was this pressure that actually helped me push through the anxiety and get it done. Optional stuff would sit in the back of my mind as something I 'should' do, which honestly just added to my stress. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually because a peer recommended it or I was genuinely curious, not because I had spare time.
Q9: Absolutely, I downloaded a lot of materials to study offline. I'd save videos and PDFs to my laptop so I could review them without needing internet or being tempted by other tabs. I also used YouTube tutorials and external resources when the course materials weren't clicking for me. My notes were all handwritten because drawing diagrams and visual maps helped me remember things better than typing ever could.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely from my downloaded notes and printed diagrams without touching the LMS at all. I'd spend hours going over flashcards I made or working through problems on paper. My peer study group would meet in person sometimes, and we'd work through concepts together without any of that showing up online. Some of my best learning happened with a whiteboard and markers, not a computer screen.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my preferred method. I'd access a video lecture once, download it or take detailed visual notes, and then never watch it on the platform again. The LMS would show one view, but I might have reviewed that content five or six times offline. It was more efficient for me to work from my own organized notes than to navigate through the platform repeatedly, especially when my anxiety made the interface feel overwhelming.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially around midterms when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, unable to start anything even though I knew I needed to. Some personal stuff happened that I won't get into, but it made focusing on coursework nearly impossible for about two weeks. The peer support helped me get through those moments, but it was still incredibly hard to stay engaged.
Q13: Workload absolutely affected my LMS usage. When I was overwhelmed, I'd only do the bare minimum online and rely more on my offline study methods. Fatigue from poor sleep due to stress meant some days I'd log in but barely process anything. I have a part-time job too, so competing responsibilities meant I had to be strategic about where I spent my energy. The confusion from juggling nine courses sometimes made me avoid the LMS entirely because it felt like too much.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other deadlines or personal stuff. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but nothing was actually sinking in. The anxiety made it hard to be present even when I was technically 'engaged' with the content. Those sessions felt like a waste, but I didn't know how else to cope.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because the idea of putting my thoughts out there for others to judge made my anxiety worse. Even when I knew participating would help my grade, I'd find excuses to delay. Some readings I avoided because they seemed too dense and I was already struggling to keep up. It wasn't laziness; it was more like self-preservation when I was already running on empty.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS activity. I'd see a due date approaching and suddenly find the motivation to log in and engage. Without deadlines, I probably would have let things slide even more than I did. The stress of deadlines was awful for my anxiety, but they also gave me structure that I desperately needed. I became very good at calculating exactly how much time I needed to complete things just before they were due.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on me, maybe too much. Positive feedback would boost my confidence and make me want to engage more immediately. Negative or critical feedback would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for a day or two while I processed it. The peer support group helped me interpret feedback more constructively, but my initial reaction was always emotional. Good feedback on assignments made me more likely to start the next one sooner.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag for me. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan, but the sheer volume across nine courses was overwhelming. I appreciated when instructors front-loaded important information in videos rather than burying it in text. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, but they did push me to interact with peers which ended up being valuable. A more flexible structure might have helped with my anxiety, but I also might have fallen behind without the rigid deadlines.
Q19: Visual elements like infographics and video explanations made engagement much easier for me. When content was text-heavy with no visual breaks, I'd struggle to stay focused. The navigation of the LMS itself was sometimes confusing, which added unnecessary stress when I was already anxious. Courses that had clear weekly checklists were easier to manage than ones where I had to hunt for what was due. The peer interaction features were helpful once I figured out how to use them properly.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking the platform without actually being able to focus on completing work. They might be logging in repeatedly, watching videos, reading discussions, but the stress prevents them from producing anything. It could also be someone who's struggling to understand the material and keeps reviewing without feeling ready to submit. I've definitely had moments like that where my activity was high but my output was low because I was stuck in an anxiety loop.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. A lot of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. My LMS activity might look low, but I was studying those materials extensively in ways that didn't register online. Some students are just more efficient and don't need to constantly be on the platform to do well. For me, less time on the LMS sometimes meant more focused, productive study time elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and how long you spend on pages. They're good at tracking completion of specific tasks and participation in discussions. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and whether someone is actually learning or just going through the motions. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or the depth of understanding. My anxiety and the effort it took to push through it would never show up in any log.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just getting through it. It's when I'm curious and asking questions, making connections between concepts, and feeling like the learning matters. For me, it also involves interacting with peers and feeling like part of a learning community. True engagement is when I walk away from a study session feeling like I grew, even if that session happened entirely offline with my notes and a cup of coffee.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much anxiety I pushed through just to submit an assignment, or the hours I spent with my peer support group working through concepts together in person. It wouldn't capture the mental health battles that made some weeks incredibly difficult, or the resilience it took to keep going despite feeling overwhelmed. My visual learning style meant a lot of my studying involved drawing diagrams and creating mind maps by hand. The data might show a moderately engaged student, but it would miss the full story of someone fighting hard to succeed despite significant internal obstacles.
